---
title: "An expansion of Causal SHAP—not a new label"
subtitle: "What carries forward, what this project adds, and which published work already owns the foundations"
toc: true
toc-depth: 2
---

::: {.page-lead}
The intellectual through-line is simple: **predictive attribution is not
intervention leverage**. Our earlier Causal SHAP work made that mismatch visible.
This project turns it into a sharper target-recovery experiment and points toward
feasibility-aware recommendations.
:::

## What carries forward from the ACIC work

- Features close to an outcome can receive predictive credit that should not be
  read as causal leverage.
- A defensible DAG and domain expertise are model inputs, not decorative
  overlays.
- Ordinary, DAG-constrained, and structurally informed attribution should be
  compared on the same learner and the same simulated truth.
- Sensitivity and negative findings belong in the headline result.

## What Target DAGs adds

<div class="lineage-grid">
  <section><span>01</span><h3>A public target DAG</h3><p>The source-aligned NASA renal-stone topology replaces a small generic graph as the larger test bed.</p></section>
  <section><span>02</span><h3>Frozen intervention truth</h3><p>Common-random-number total effects provide a known ranking for every eligible ancestor.</p></section>
  <section><span>03</span><h3>A matched negative control</h3><p>DAG ordering is tested separately from intervention propagation—and is null.</p></section>
  <section><span>04</span><h3>Structural coalition values</h3><p>Unfixed descendants respond to $do(X_S=x_S)$ rather than remaining a static feature background.</p></section>
  <section><span>05</span><h3>Target-recovery metrics</h3><p>Rank recovery, top-$k$ overlap, DAG distance, PBI, and POA quantify where attribution mass lands.</p></section>
  <section><span>06</span><h3>A bridge to action</h3><p>Effect ranking becomes one input to feasible, cost-aware, uncertainty-aware intervention choice.</p></section>
</div>

The concise lineage note is preserved in
[`docs/ACIC_LINEAGE.md`](https://github.com/andystats/causal-shap-target-dags/blob/main/docs/ACIC_LINEAGE.md).

## The published foundation we can leverage

### 1. The question is causal

[Janzing, Minorics & Blöbaum (AISTATS 2020)](https://proceedings.mlr.press/v108/janzing20a.html)
show that disagreement over SHAP background distributions is really a failure
to distinguish observation from intervention. This supports our opening move:
say exactly whether we are explaining a model input or a real-world change.

### 2. Causal Shapley values already propagate interventions

[Heskes, Sijben, Bucur & Claassen (NeurIPS 2020)](https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper/2020/file/32e54441e6382a7fbacbbbaf3c450059-Paper.pdf)
define the do-based coalition value used here and show how total Shapley
contribution can be decomposed into direct and indirect parts. Their chain
example is especially close to our Markov explanation: a predictor can ignore
an upstream feature while a causal Shapley value recovers its propagated
contribution.

### 3. Causal ordering is a distinct design choice

[Frye, Rowat & Feige (NeurIPS 2020)](https://papers.nips.cc/paper_files/paper/2020/hash/0d770c496aa3da6d2c3f2bd19e7b9d6b-Abstract.html)
introduce asymmetric Shapley values by restricting permutations to respect known
causal structure. Our NASA-topology null does not contradict that contribution.
It establishes a narrower empirical point: **ordering coalitions did not recover
total-effect targets in this simulation unless the value function also
propagated interventions.**

### 4. Recommendation requires recourse, not just explanation

[Karimi, Schölkopf & Valera (FAccT 2021)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2002.06278)
argue for moving from nearest counterfactual explanations to minimal causal
interventions: from “where is a favorable case?” to “what action gets there?”
Their companion
[NeurIPS 2020 paper](https://proceedings.neurips.cc/paper/2020/file/02a3c7fb3f489288ae6942498498db20-Paper.pdf)
adds uncertainty over imperfect structural knowledge. This is the right
published bridge from our target ranking to intervenable recommendations.

### 5. Known-truth validation should be designed, not improvised

[Parikh et al. (ICML 2022)](https://proceedings.mlr.press/v162/parikh22a.html)
provide the Credence framework for validating causal inference methods against
designed data-generating processes. Our layered synthetic validation follows
that spirit while using the author’s own implementation.

## The defensible novelty claim

This project should **not** claim a new Causal SHAP definition. A more coherent
claim is:

::: {.claim-box}
We provide a reproducible target-recovery demonstration showing why Markov
screening concentrates predictive SHAP near an outcome, why causal ordering
alone can leave that failure intact, and why an intervention-propagating
coalition value is the relevant bridge toward actionable target ranking.
:::

That claim is both more modest and more useful. It connects established theory
to an auditable evaluation question: *did the method recover the levers whose
interventions are known to move the target?*

## The next research step

The next stage is not another attribution variant. It is a constrained action
problem:

1. estimate structural effects under a set of plausible DAGs and mechanisms;
2. define manipulable variables, action ranges, costs, and ethical exclusions;
3. optimize expected improvement subject to a robustness threshold;
4. validate the resulting policy in simulation before any substantive use.

::: {.next-step}
[Return to the focused demonstration](index.qmd) or
[inspect the current evidence](evidence.qmd).
:::
